summary:
The Bronze Age Collapse, occurring roughly between 1200 and 1150 BC, was one of the most catastrophic civilizational disruptions in recorded history. Within a span of a few decades, virtually every major palace civilization around the eastern Mediterranean either collapsed entirely or contracted dramatically. The [[event:54|Hittite Empire]] was destroyed. The [[event:57|Mycenaean civilization]] fell. The great trading city of [[event:1833|Ugarit]] was burned and abandoned. Egyptian power contracted severely under pressure from the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]]. The prosperous palace economies of Cyprus were shattered. Trade networks that had integrated the entire region for centuries ceased to function.

The causes of the Bronze Age Collapse remain vigorously debated among archaeologists and historians, and most contemporary scholars favor a multicausal explanation in which no single factor was decisive. The leading hypotheses include the invasions of the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]]; severe droughts and famines, supported by paleoclimatic evidence from lake sediment cores showing a dramatic rainfall decline around 1200 BC; systems collapse resulting from the fragility of the highly interdependent Bronze Age palace economies; internal rebellions by subject populations; and earthquakes. A 2013 study proposed that cascading failures across the interconnected Bronze Age system — each crisis triggering further collapse — best explain the synchronicity of destruction across such a wide area.

The evidence for catastrophic climate change is among the most compelling elements of the collapse narrative. Pollen records, isotope analysis, and ancient letters from [[event:1833|Ugarit]] requesting emergency grain shipments indicate severe, multi-year droughts in the decades around 1200 BC. If agricultural failure disrupted the grain surpluses that supported palace bureaucracies, armies, and specialist craftsmen, the entire Bronze Age redistributive economic system could have unraveled.

The human consequences were profound. Population levels across the eastern Mediterranean declined dramatically. The [[event:1826|Greek Dark Ages]] that followed the Mycenaean collapse saw dramatic reductions in settlement size, loss of literacy, and cessation of long-distance trade. The [[event:1823|Linear B]] writing system was lost entirely. Egyptian power contracted to the Nile valley. Dozens of cities were abandoned and never reoccupied. The complexity and interconnectedness of the Late Bronze Age world would not be matched again until centuries later.

The Bronze Age Collapse is significant not only as a historical catastrophe but as a case study in civilizational vulnerability. Modern scholars studying systemic risk, network fragility, and the dynamics of collapse have found the Bronze Age example instructive for thinking about the resilience and fragility of complex, interconnected societies — a concern with obvious contemporary relevance.
